summary: Evans describes the kernel she wrote at Hacker School by listing everything it can't do, using that as a framework for explaining what kernels actually handle. Her kernel can only print to the screen, understand keyboard inputs, and almost run programs. By enumerating missing capabilities like filesystem support, networking, scheduling, and memory protection, she makes the scope of kernel work concrete and approachable. She's preparing a conference talk arguing that kernel programming is something normal humans can do, and frames the learning process as picking a specific subsystem and diving in.
key_points: A toy kernel that can only print to screen and read keyboard input reveals how much work production kernels do
Listing what's missing (filesystems, TCP/IP, scheduling, memory protection) makes kernel concepts concrete rather than abstract
Understanding what a kernel does lets you pick any subsystem and dive into real kernel source code productively
Kernel programming has become concrete enough that she feels she could write a production OS given enough time and help
money_quotes: I now totally feel like I could write a production OS if I were given some hardware, 20 years and an army of volunteers.
The idea is that once you know what a kernel does, you can pick a Thing and a Kernel, and then dive into it.
These are all pretty approachable concepts (I think).
I'm working on a talk for CUSEC about how kernel programming is something that normal humans can do (albeit with some pain and suffering).
key_insight: Building a minimal kernel that can barely do anything is a powerful way to demystify what production kernels actually handle, making the whole domain feel approachable.
